parts.name: Valve Cover Gasket

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Valve cover's off. Yep — oil seeping past both cam seals. Classic 4A-GE.

flavorText: Gasket's toast. Classic 4A-GE move.
Oil weeping down the side of the head. This thing's been crying for years.
Cork gasket, naturally. Toyota loved these until they didn't.

parts.name: Cam Seals (Intake & Exhaust)

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Seals are out. Hang on — there's visible wear on the cam journals. That's not great.

flavorText: Both seals are hard as a hockey puck. No wonder they're leaking.
Oil everywhere once these go. Cheap part, fiddly install.
Gotta get the seal seated square or it'll leak again in a month.

parts.name: Camshaft Journal Wear
flavorText: Scoring on the journals. She's been run low on oil at some point.
Cam lobes still look okay, but these journals need attention.
Not the worst I've seen, but you'll feel it in the idle if you leave it.

parts.name: Valve Adjustment Shims
flavorText: Shim-over-bucket setup. Measure twice, shim once.
Couple of these are way out of spec. You can hear it ticking.
Grab the feeler gauges. This is where patience pays off.

parts.name: Front Crankshaft Seal
flavorText: Oil slinging off the crank pulley. Seal's done.
With the timing belt off you can see it weeping. Swap it now or regret it later.
Cheap seal, but you gotta pull the timing gear to get to it.

parts.name: Rear Main Seal
flavorText: The one seal everyone dreads. Trans has to come out to get here.
Oil dripping off the bell housing. Rear main, guaranteed.
While you're in there, just do it. You don't want to pull the trans twice.

parts.name: Piston Rings (Set of 4)
flavorText: Compression test told the story. Rings are shot on cylinders 2 and 3.
Oil consumption and blow-by. These rings have seen better decades.
Ring gaps are way out of spec. Time for a fresh set and a hone.

parts.name: Timing Belt

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Belt's off. The tensioner has play in it — grab it and wiggle. Yeah, that's no good.

flavorText: Cracked and glazed. This belt's been on here since the Clinton administration.
The 4A-GE is interference. If this snaps, the valves meet the pistons. Not a friendly meeting.
Always do the belt, tensioner, and water pump together. Don't be cheap.

parts.name: Timing Belt Tensioner
flavorText: Bearing's crunchy. Spin it by hand — sounds like gravel.
If the tensioner goes, it takes the belt with it. Replace together, always.
Spring's lost tension. This thing was barely keeping the belt on.

parts.name: Water Pump

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pump's out. Gasket surface underneath is corroded — you'll need to clean that up and reseal.

flavorText: Weep hole is dripping. She's on her way out.
Timing belt is right there — do the pump while you're in it. No excuses.
Impeller's still intact but the bearing's got play. Swap it.

parts.name: Water Pump Housing Gasket
flavorText: Paper gasket, crusty as expected. Scrape the surface clean before the new one goes on.
Twelve-dollar part that'll cause a hundred-dollar headache if you skip it.
Use a razor blade and patience. Don't gouge the aluminum.

parts.name: Throttle Body

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Throttle body's off and — oh. Now you can see the vacuum line spaghetti behind it. Some of these are cracked to pieces.

flavorText: Butterfly valve's gummed up. Carb cleaner and a rag, she'll clean up.
Bore's full of carbon. Somebody never changed the air filter.
Throttle cable's sticky too. Hit everything with cleaner while it's apart.

parts.name: Vacuum Lines
flavorText: Half these lines are cracked. The other half are plugged with someone's idea of a fix.
Vacuum spaghetti. Trace every line, label before you pull. Trust me.
The 4A-GE vacuum routing diagram should be on the hood. It's not. Of course.

parts.name: Intake Manifold Gasket

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Manifold's off. Look at the intake ports — oil pooling in runners 2 and 3. Rings are letting blow-by into the intake side.

flavorText: Sucking air past the gasket. You can hear it hiss at idle.
Manifold bolts are all different lengths because someone's been here before. Fun.
Spray some carb cleaner around the base. If the idle changes, you found your leak.

parts.name: Idle Air Control Valve
flavorText: Idle's hunting like crazy. IACV's gunked up or dead.
Pull it off and blast it with cleaner first. Sometimes that's all it needs.
Plunger is stuck. Carbon buildup all over. This car sat for a long time.

parts.name: Fuel Injector #1
flavorText: Spray pattern's all wrong. This one's dribbling, not atomizing.
O-ring's hard. Injector might clean up, but the seal needs replacing either way.
Ohm it out — resistance is within spec but it's still clogged.

parts.name: Fuel Injector #2
flavorText: Dead injector. Cylinder two's running lean and it shows.
Pintle's stuck. Soak it overnight, sometimes they come back.
Click test: nothing. This one's electrically dead.

parts.name: Fuel Injector #3
flavorText: Varnish all over the tip. Old gas did a number on this one.
Flow rate's about 60% of the others. That's your misfire right there.
Could try an ultrasonic bath, but at this point just replace all four.

parts.name: Fuel Injector #4
flavorText: This one actually looks okay. Clean it, new O-ring, back in it goes.
Spray pattern's decent. Best of the four, which isn't saying much.
Lucky number four. At least one thing in this fuel system isn't trashed.

parts.name: Fuel Rail
flavorText: Rail's got some surface corrosion but it's structurally fine. Clean and paint it.
Pull the rail with all four injectors still in. Way easier to service on the bench.
Fuel residue everywhere. Pop the clips, pull steady, and have a rag ready.

parts.name: Fuel Filter
flavorText: This filter's darker than coffee. No telling when it was last changed.
Twenty-dollar part, ten-minute job. No reason not to do it.
Check the flow direction arrow. Someone installed the last one backwards.

parts.name: Fuel Pressure Regulator
flavorText: Diaphragm's leaking. Pull the vacuum line — if fuel comes out, there's your answer.
Fuel pressure's all over the place. Regulator's lost its mind.
Vacuum reference line is cracked too. Replace both while you're here.

parts.name: Fuel Pump
flavorText: Key on — listen. That whine is weak. Pump's dying.
Fuel pump's in the tank on these. Drop the tank or go through the trunk.
Pressure test says 28 PSI. Spec is 38. This pump's tired.

parts.name: Radiator

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Radiator's out. The lower hose behind it is ballooned and soft — that's a blowout waiting to happen.

flavorText: Fins are bent to hell and half the tubes are green with corrosion.
Someone tried to JB Weld the end tank. Points for effort.
Plastic end tanks on an aluminum core. They all crack eventually.

parts.name: Thermostat
flavorText: Stuck closed. Boil test confirmed it — this thing's a paperweight.
Fifteen bucks and twenty minutes. Easiest fix on the whole car.
Housing bolts are corroded. Hit 'em with penetrating oil first.

parts.name: Upper Radiator Hose
flavorText: Squeeze it. If it doesn't spring back, it's done.
Hose is swollen near the thermostat housing. Heat cycles got it.
New clamps too. The old ones are one-time-use spring clips.

parts.name: Lower Radiator Hose
flavorText: Collapsing under suction at high RPM. Internal spring is gone.
Tucked behind the radiator. Pain to reach but not a hard swap.
Soft as a wet noodle. Coolant's been eating it from the inside.

parts.name: Heater Hoses
flavorText: Routed through the firewall. One's cracked right at the heater core fitting.
No heat in the cabin? Start here before you blame the heater core.
These always get forgotten. Out of sight, out of mind, until your feet are wet.

parts.name: Radiator Cap
flavorText: Spring's weak. Not holding pressure — that's why it's puking coolant.
Rubber seal is cracked and flat. Ten bucks to fix an overheating mystery.
Pressure test the cap separately. If it doesn't hold 13 PSI, toss it.

parts.name: Exhaust Manifold / Header

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Header's off. The exhaust manifold gasket is blown between cylinders 2 and 3. That explains the ticking.

flavorText: Studs are rusted in. Get the torch ready, this is a fight.
Hairline crack near the collector. You can hear the exhaust leak on cold start.
Four-into-one design. Toyota kept it simple but the studs didn't get the memo.

parts.name: Exhaust Manifold Gasket
flavorText: Blown between two ports. You can see daylight through the gasket.
Multi-layer steel gasket, but it's been cooking for forty years.
Fifteen-dollar fix that sounds like a five-hundred-dollar problem.

parts.name: Catalytic Converter
flavorText: Rattle it. Hear that? Substrate's broken apart inside.
Honeycomb is melted — someone was running rich for a long time.
It's clogged. Backpressure's killing power. This has to go.

parts.name: Mid-Pipe
flavorText: Rusted through right where it bends. Classic rust belt special.
Flange bolts are seized. Penetrating oil and prayer.
Hangers are gone too. It's been resting on the driveshaft tunnel.

parts.name: Muffler
flavorText: Internal baffles are shot. Sounds like a tin can full of bees.
Rusted at the seam. Poke it with a screwdriver — goes right through.
Someone welded a coffee can over the last hole. Respect the hustle.

parts.name: O2 Sensor
flavorText: Sensor's lazy. Response time is way too slow — running rich because of it.
Connector's corroded. Clean it first, but this sensor's probably cooked.
Forty years of exhaust gas. The tip looks like it's been dipped in tar.

parts.name: Clutch Disc

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Clutch is out and — yep, rear main seal is weeping oil all over the flywheel. That's why it was slipping.

flavorText: Friction material is glazed smooth. This clutch has been slipping for a while.
Down to the rivets on the inside. Surprised it still grabs at all.
Trans has to come out. Clear your afternoon.

parts.name: Pressure Plate
flavorText: Fingers are worn unevenly. This thing wasn't releasing properly.
Hot spots on the friction surface. It's been cooking itself.
Always replace with the disc. They wear as a set.

parts.name: Throw-Out Bearing
flavorText: Chirps when you push the clutch pedal. Dead giveaway.
Spin it by hand. That grinding? Yeah, new bearing time.
Thirty-five bucks. You'd be insane not to replace it while the trans is out.

parts.name: Flywheel Resurface
flavorText: Heat cracks all over the surface. Machine shop can save it if it's not too deep.
Glazed but no hot spots. Quick resurface and it's good to go.
Check the ring gear teeth while it's out. Starter's been chewing on them.

parts.name: Rear Differential
flavorText: Whining on decel. Ring and pinion are on their way out.
Drain the fluid — if it sparkles, the gears are eating themselves.
The AE86 open diff. Everyone wants an LSD swap. This one just wants fresh oil.

parts.name: Driveshaft Carrier Bearing
flavorText: Vibration at highway speed. Grab the driveshaft — if it clunks, carrier bearing's dead.
Rubber isolator is crumbling. Bearing itself might be fine, but the mount is toast.
Two-piece driveshaft on these. The center bearing takes all the abuse.

parts.name: CV Axles (Pair)
flavorText: Boots are torn, grease everywhere. The joints are clicking on turns.
One's got play. The other's making noise. Replace both, save the headache.
Grease slung all over the inside of the wheel. Boots have been gone a while.

parts.name: Front Brake Pads

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Pads are out. The rotors behind them are scored deep — you can feel the grooves with your fingernail.

flavorText: Metal on metal. The wear indicators are long gone.
Inner pad's down to the backing plate. Outer's got a little left. Caliper's sticking.
At least these are easy. Two bolts and a C-clamp.

parts.name: Front Brake Rotors
flavorText: Lip on the edge you could cut yourself on. Way past minimum thickness.
Warped. You can feel the pulsing through the pedal on every stop.
Scored deep enough to catch a thumbnail. New rotors, not a resurface.

parts.name: Rear Brake Shoes

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Shoes are out. The drum surface is grooved and there's a hot spot from the shoes dragging. Drums gotta go.

flavorText: Springs are a puzzle. Take a picture before you pull anything apart.
Shoe material is crumbling. One side is soaked in brake fluid — leaking wheel cylinder.
Drum brakes. Welcome to 1985. The self-adjuster is seized, naturally.

parts.name: Rear Brake Drums
flavorText: Out of round. You can see the wear ridge without a micrometer.
Drums are stuck on. Couple persuasive taps with a mallet should do it.
Scored past the max diameter stamp. Can't turn these, gotta replace.

parts.name: Brake Lines
flavorText: Rubber flex lines are swollen. Hard lines have rust scale on them.
Spongy pedal? Start here. These hoses are expanding under pressure.
Flare nuts are seized. Get a proper flare nut wrench or you'll round them.

parts.name: Brake Master Cylinder
flavorText: Pedal goes to the floor. Internal seals are bypassing.
Bench bleed the new one before you install it. Saves hours of bleeding.
Fluid's black. When's the last time someone flushed this system? Never?

parts.name: Brake Fluid Flush
flavorText: Fluid's brown. It absorbs moisture and cooks the seals. Flush it.
Grab a buddy — one pumps, one bleeds. Start at the furthest wheel.
DOT 3 or DOT 4. Read the cap, not the internet.

parts.name: Front Struts

hiddenReveal.flavorText: Strut's out. The spring's sagging — one side sits lower than the other. No wonder it pulls.

flavorText: Bounce test: three bounces before it settles. Should be one. Struts are blown.
Oil leaking down the body. These are done — riding on the bump stops.
McPherson struts up front. Spring compressor required. Don't skip it.

parts.name: Front Springs
flavorText: One spring has a broken coil at the bottom. That clunk over bumps? Found it.
Sagged about an inch compared to spec. Car's been sitting on these for decades.
Spring compressor time. Be careful — stored energy in these will ruin your day.

parts.name: Tie Rod Ends
flavorText: Grab and wiggle. Clunk. That's your steering slop right there.
Boot's torn and the ball joint's dry. Replace both sides.
Count the threads before you take 'em off. Gets your alignment close enough to drive to the shop.

parts.name: Front Ball Joints
flavorText: Jack it up, pry bar under the tire. If it moves, ball joint's shot.
Worn ball joints make the whole front end feel vague. These are cooked.
Pickle fork or press — your call. Either way, the boot's trashed.

parts.name: Front Control Arm Bushings
flavorText: Rubber's cracked and separating from the sleeve. Zero compliance left.
Every bump feels like a suggestion, not a command. Bushings are the reason.
Press 'em out, press 'em in. Alignment after. No shortcuts.

parts.name: Steering Rack Boots
flavorText: Torn wide open. Dirt and water getting into the rack. Replace before the rack dies.
Cheap insurance. Keeps the rack alive for another few decades.
Zip-tie style or clamp style — either works. Just get them on tight.

parts.name: Rear Shocks
flavorText: Oil stains running down the shock body. They've given up.
Rear end hops over bumps instead of settling. Shocks have zero damping left.
Four-link rear on the AE86. Shocks are straightforward — two bolts each.

parts.name: Rear Springs
flavorText: Sagging. You can tell by the rear wheel gap — it's sitting low.
Coil springs in the back. One has a rust pit that's going to become a crack.
Measure ride height side to side. If they don't match, at least one spring is tired.

parts.name: Sway Bar Bushings
flavorText: Clunking over speed bumps. Bushings are oval — bar's just rattling around in there.
Polyurethane or rubber, your call. Either way these originals are done.
Twenty-dollar fix that makes the car feel ten years newer in corners.

systems.name: Interior
flavorText: Cracked dash, torn seats, shift boot's in shreds. Full 80s Toyota interior experience.
The dash is a moonscape of cracks. Seat foam is visible through the cloth. But the gauge cluster still works — Toyota quality where it counts.
Smells like old oil and sun-baked vinyl. This interior tells a story. Not a great one.

systems.name: Electrical
flavorText: Alternator's weak, starter solenoid clicks sometimes, and somebody spliced into the harness for a stereo that's long gone.
Fusible links are crusty. Half the dash lights are out. The wiring harness has three colors of electrical tape in it.
Check the grounds first. On old Toyotas, it's always the grounds.

systems.name: Body
flavorText: Quarter panels are bubbly with rust. Fender flares are cracked. The paint is checking everywhere.
Rust in the usual spots — rear wheel wells, rocker panels, battery tray. Gonna need metal work.
She's straight at least. No accident damage. Just forty years of weather and parking lot dings.

systems.name: Glass & Seals
flavorText: Windshield seal is letting water in. Rear glass seal is dried out. Cabin smells like mildew when it rains.
Rubber seals are hard as rocks. The glass is fine but everything around it is failing.
Pop-up headlight covers are hazed. Windshield has a long crack. Quarter glass seals are shot.

systems.name: Exterior Trim
flavorText: Bumpers are faded, mirrors are cracked, and someone lost a door handle on the passenger side.
Weatherstripping is flat and hard. Door seals don't seal. Wind noise at 40 mph.
All the rubber and plastic bits that make a car look finished instead of abandoned.

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Engine idles rough, stalls at stop lights

free: RPM fluctuates between 500 and 1200 at idle
No check engine light, no obvious vacuum leaks visible
clues.multimeter: Idle air control valve resistance reads open circuit

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Car overheats sitting in traffic but runs cool on the highway

free: Temperature gauge climbs quickly at red lights
Coolant level is full, no visible leaks

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Ticking noise on cold start that fades as engine warms up

free: Tick is loudest near the exhaust manifold area
No tick after about 5 minutes of running
clues.compressionTester: Compression is even across all four cylinders — not an internal issue

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: RPMs climb but the car doesn't accelerate in third gear under load

free: Burning smell after hard acceleration
Clutch pedal engagement point is very high, near the top of travel

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: New clutch installed but it's already slipping intermittently after 50 miles

free: Oil drips visible on the bell housing
Clutch disc friction surface has oil staining

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Uneven idle and a faint knocking from the head at low RPM that gets worse when the engine is warm

free: Knocking is audible with a stethoscope on the valve cover, loudest near the cam journals
Oil pressure drops slightly at hot idle — gauge reads lower than normal
clues.compressionTester: Compression is even across all four cylinders — bottom end is fine

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Idle hangs high after blipping the throttle and the car hesitates on light acceleration

free: Spraying carb cleaner around the intake changes the idle speed
You can hear a faint hissing near the back of the intake manifold
clues.multimeter: O2 sensor voltage is pegged lean — 0.1V steady at idle

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Car runs rich at idle with black smoke, but stumbles and bogs down under hard acceleration

free: Spark plugs are sooty black on all four cylinders
Fuel smell is strong at the exhaust tip even after warm-up
clues.multimeter: Injector pulse width looks normal — ECU is commanding correctly

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Fuel economy has tanked and the car smells rich, but there's no obvious misfire or power loss

free: Exhaust has a rotten egg smell that wasn't there before
No visible leaks in the exhaust system — everything is sealed
clues.multimeter: O2 sensor voltage is stuck at 0.45V — no switching between rich and lean

diagnosticScenarios.symptom: Headlights dim at idle, the starter cranks slowly on cold mornings, and the voltmeter reads low

free: Battery is less than a year old — tested good at the parts store
There's a rats nest of spliced wires behind the dash from a stereo install
clues.multimeter: Alternator output is 11.8V at idle — should be 13.5-14.5V. Ground strap resistance is 4 ohms — way too high

firstStartText: You flip the key. The starter cranks — once, twice — and the 4A-GE catches. That distinctive twin-cam wail rises through the rev range. Four throttle bodies gulping air, sixteen valves dancing. She's alive. Takumi would be proud.
loreBlurb: The lightweight Corolla that became a drift legend. Powered by Toyota's jewel-like 4A-GE twin cam, the AE86 proved that momentum and driver skill beat horsepower.
